title-group.article-title: Cell biomechanics on muscle atrophy: from intricate mechanisms to therapeutic frontiers

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Muscle atrophy—the decline of skeletal muscle volume and function—is pervasive in chronic disease, aging, and inactivity. As the primary driver of human mobility and metabolic health, skeletal muscle loss diminishes quality of life and increases healthcare burden. Atrophy impairs recovery and prognosis by reducing metabolic capacity, accelerating systemic protein catabolism, and compromising the biomechanical support necessary for movement and respiration. Although core molecular pathways and cellular changes are well characterized, the role of mechanical cues in modulating these mechanisms remains underexplored.

sec.title: Main Findings
sec.p: Our review reveals five convergent atrophy drivers—mechanical unloading, ECM alterations, mitochondrial dysfunction/oxidative stress, inflammation, and endocrine imbalance—that converge on inhibited mTORC1 signaling, activated FoxO/UPS/autophagy, and impaired satellite‐cell function. Quantitative data show that axial stretch preserves PI3K/Akt/mTOR activity, with phosphorylated Akt levels increasing by two‐ to three‐fold and fiber cross‐sectional area expanding by 10%–20%; low‐intensity compression activates AMPK and autophagy, with AMPK phosphorylation rising by 1.5‐fold without triggering excessive protein breakdown; and shear stress enhances VEGF and Nrf2‐mediated angiogenesis and antioxidant defenses, doubling VEGF expression and reducing ROS levels by 25% to mitigate neurogenic atrophy. Moreover, stem‐cell myogenic differentiation is optimized on 3D biomimetic substrates with stiffness from 11 to 17 kPa under physiological loading, and advances in biomaterials and tissue engineering enable more accurate muscle‐tissue models.

sec.title: Future Directions
sec.p: Translating these biomechanical insights into tailored clinical interventions—combining stretch, compression, and shear modalities with biomaterials, stem-cell technologies, and personalized exercise programs— holds promise for preventing and reversing muscle atrophy across diverse patient populations.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Muscle atrophy refers to the gradual decline in the volume and function of skeletal muscles and is a common phenomenon associated with various disease states, aging, and prolonged inactivity, such as bed rest or microgravity environments [1]. Given that skeletal muscles play a central role in human movement, metabolic regulation, and overall health maintenance, muscle atrophy not only significantly diminishes quality of life but also exacerbates the burden on social and healthcare systems. Research by HRDY O et al. indicates that the incidence of muscle atrophy among intensive care unit (ICU) patients is approximately 55%, while the incidence of ICU-acquired weakness is about 45%. The thickness of the rectus femoris and quadriceps muscles decreased by an average of 1.75% and 1.82% per day during the first week of admission to the intensive care unit [2]. In addition, studies have shown that sarcopenia, or age-related muscle atrophy, is an important issue affecting the ability of older adults to live independently and the need for long-term care for older adults in the context of the global trend toward aging [3]. Muscle atrophy is also strongly associated with many clinicopathologic processes, such as neurologic injuries (e.g. stroke and spinal cord injuries) [4], chronic diseases (e.g. diabetes mellitus, cancer cachexia, and chronic renal disease) [5], and changes in the external environment (e.g. prolonged bed rest or microgravity environments) [6]. These factors have a significant impact on the long-term prognosis and speed of recovery of patients. Muscle atrophy includes significant changes at the cellular and molecular level in addition to degeneration of physiologic components. Reduced mechanical stress, changes in the extracellular matrix, malfunction of muscle stem cells, and an imbalance between protein production and degradation have all been linked to muscle atrophy, according to studies [7].

To date, numerous studies have explored the genetic, metabolic, and pharmacological mechanisms contributing to muscle atrophy. However, the biomechanical regulatory systems—particularly how muscle cells perceive and transduce mechanical signals—remain incompletely understood. This is despite increasing evidence that mechanical stimuli play a vital role in muscle development, homeostasis, and degeneration [8]. The absence or alteration of mechanical cues, such as in disuse or microgravity environments, has been shown to accelerate muscle loss, yet the underlying mechanotransduction pathways and biomechanical feedback mechanisms have not been fully elucidated. This knowledge gap hampers the development of targeted therapeutic strategies that incorporate mechanical regulation as a core principle.

To clarify the role of mechanical signals in regulating muscle atrophy, this review synthesizes recent advancements in the biomechanical mechanisms underlying muscle loss. It particularly focuses on mechanotransduction pathways and emerging therapeutic approaches, including exercise interventions, stem cell-based therapies, and biomaterial applications. By enhancing our understanding of how mechanical environments influence muscle pathophysiology, this work seeks to provide a theoretical foundation for the development of novel biomechanics-oriented strategies for the prevention and treatment of muscle atrophy.

sec.title: Pathophysiology and mechanisms of muscle atrophy
sec.p: Skeletal muscle homeostasis relies on mechanical loading, neural input, mitochondrial energy production, immune-inflammatory balance, and endocrine signals to maintain a net equilibrium between protein synthesis and degradation. Under normal loading conditions, such as exercise, integrin-mediated mechanotransduction activates the PI3K/Akt/mTOR pathway, promoting ribosomal biogenesis and protein translation while inhibiting FoxO-dependent E3 ligases (MuRF1, Atrogin-1) and autophagy genes, thereby preserving myofibrillar integrity [9,10]. Conversely, when mechanical stress is reduced, such as during immobilization or bed rest, mTOR signaling is suppressed, and FoxO is activated, which accelerates the ubiquitin–proteasome and autophagy–lysosome-mediated degradation of myofibrils, leading to a net loss of protein [11–13]. Simultaneously, diminished loading or denervation impairs mitochondrial biogenesis, reduces oxidative enzyme activity, and elevates reactive oxygen species (ROS), which activate NF-κB and p38 MAPK, further inhibiting mTOR and promoting proteasomal degradation [14,15]. Damaged mitochondria may also release pro-apoptotic factors that hasten myofiber apoptosis [16,17]. In chronic disease or injury settings, circulating TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6, and IFN-γ infiltrate muscle tissue, activating NF-κB/STAT3 in myofibers to upregulate MuRF1 and Atrogin-1 while suppressing mTOR, disrupting satellite cell Notch/Wnt signalling, thereby impairing regeneration and favouring fibrosis or adipogenesis [17–20]. Endocrine disturbances, including elevated glucocorticoids, thyroid dysfunction, and age-related declines in IGF-1, testosterone, and estrogen, further inhibit mTOR and enhance FoxO-driven proteolysis, weakening satellite cell proliferation and differentiation [21–23]. Collectively, these interrelated disruptions in mechanotransduction, mitochondrial function, inflammatory signalling, endocrine regulation, and satellite cell activity drive myofiber atrophy and muscle mass loss (Figure 1). However, different types of muscle atrophy exhibit distinct mechanistic characteristics.

caption.p: Protein synthesis (left) vs. protein degradation (right) in muscle atrophy.

Left (Synthesis): Mechanical loading, amino acids, insulin, and IGF-1 activate PI3K/Akt/mTOR, promoting ribosomal biogenesis and protein translation to maintain muscle‐protein balance. Right (Degradation): Inflammation, reduced mechanical stress, denervation, or starvation inhibit mTOR and activate FoxO, upregulating MuRF1/Atrogin-1 and autophagy genes. This drives ubiquitin–proteasome and autophagy–lysosome–mediated breakdown of myofibrillar proteins.

sec.p: Outcome: When degradation exceeds synthesis, net protein loss leads to myofiber shrinkage and muscle atrophy.

sec.title: Disuse muscle atrophy
sec.p: Myogenic muscle atrophy refers to muscle wasting that arises from intrinsic problems within muscle cells. This condition can be attributed to factors such as hereditary muscle diseases, inflammatory myopathies, metabolic myopathies, toxic and drug-induced myopathies, and sarcopenia [24]. The primary pathophysiological mechanisms involved include muscle fibre damage, degeneration, and necrosis, along with reduced muscle protein synthesis and the release of inflammatory mediators. Collectively, these mechanisms lead to a decline in muscle strength and the development of atrophy. The principal clinical manifestations include progressive muscle weakness, atrophy, alterations in muscle tone, and fatigue [25,26].

sec.title: Neurogenic atrophy
sec.p: Denervation leads to a rapid loss of integrin-FAK/mTOR signalling, disrupts Ca2+ homeostasis, activates calpains, and increases mitochondrial ROS production. These changes subsequently activate the FoxO pathway, upregulate MuRF1/Atrogin-1, and enhance autophagy, thereby accelerating protein degradation [15,16,27]. Local inflammation in denervated regions promotes ECM fibrosis, with elevated levels of both MMPs and collagen synthesis, resulting in uneven stiffness at the fibre–matrix interface [20,27]. The mechanotransduction activity of RhoA/ROCK declines, which hinders the ability of fibres to maintain tension. Consequently, core-like sarcomeric damage, fibre irregularity, loss of muscle strength, and localized functional deficits are observed [28,29].

sec.title: Myogenic atrophy
sec.p: Defects in myofibrillar structural proteins, such as the dystrophin complex, disrupt the coupling between integrins and the cytoskeleton, consequently suppressing FAK/mTOR signalling. This suppression leads to the activation of FoxO, which upregulates MuRF1 and Atrogin-1, thereby enhancing autophagy. The result is a decrease in protein synthesis and an increase in degradation [9,10,13]. Mitochondrial dysfunction and elevated levels of ROS further exacerbate NF-κB-mediated proteolysis. Additionally, chronic inflammation contributes to an increase in ECM stiffness, which impedes fibre extension [17,18]. Dysfunction in mechanosensitive channels, such as PIEZO1 and TRPC1, prevents YAP/TAZ from activating myogenic genes, thereby limiting satellite cell regeneration [20,23]. The structural consequences of these processes include core-like lesions, an increased number of centralized nuclei, heterogeneous fibre diameters, and ECM hardening, all of which lead to a marked reduction in overall stiffness and endurance [30].

sec.title: Microgravity-induced muscle atrophy
sec.p: Microgravity refers to an environment where gravitational forces are nearly negligible [31]. This condition can be achieved through the equilibrium of centrifugal force and gravity in orbit, leading to a state commonly described as ‘weightlessness,’ or through the temporary neutralization of gravity in ground-based free-fall facilities [32]. Under such conditions, the equilibrium between muscle protein synthesis and degradation is significantly disrupted. The reduction in gravitational loading inhibits mTORC1 activity, thereby decreasing protein synthesis. Concurrently, the inactivation of the Akt/FOXO axis enhances the activation of the ubiquitin–proteasome system (UPS) and the autophagy–lysosome pathway, which accelerates the breakdown of myofibrillar proteins [33,34]. The expression of Atrogin‐1 and MuRF‐1 is markedly upregulated, while the downstream phosphorylation of the PI3K/Akt/mTOR pathway decreases, further promoting proteolysis and the loss of myofibers [35]. Additionally, elevated levels of ROS and compromised antioxidant defenses activate NF-κB signalling, exacerbating inflammation and protein degradation [36]. Mitochondrial dysfunction, characterized by decreased biogenesis and ATP production, disrupts cellular energy homeostasis [37]. The absence of mechanical loading destabilizes the cytoskeletal network and disrupts sarcomere alignment, resulting in a significant decline in intracellular tension and contractile force. Mechanotransduction is further impaired as mechanosensitive channels (e.g. Piezo1) and integrin/focal adhesion complexes lose functionality, which hinders force transmission between the plasma membrane and the cytoplasm [38]. Over time, the expression of cytoskeletal proteins diminishes, and the contractile function of myofibrils deteriorates, culminating in a substantial loss of muscle strength. Data from long-duration missions conducted by NASA and ESA indicate that astronauts may experience a 10%–30% reduction in the cross-sectional area of lower-limb muscle fibres, particularly in the gastrocnemius and quadriceps, during prolonged periods in orbit [32,39].

sec.title: Cellular biomechanics and the mechanism of muscle atrophy
Changes in the mechanical properties of muscle cells
Changes in the morphology, size, and elasticity of muscle cell
sec.p: Previous studies have shown that muscle cells undergoing atrophy not only exhibit a decline in functional capacity but also undergo significant alterations in their structural and biomechanical properties. Changes in the mechanical environment directly influence the morphology, size, and physical characteristics of muscle cells. Under conditions of insufficient mechanical loading-such as prolonged bed rest, microgravity exposure, or nerve denervation-muscle cells often display morphological changes characterized by reduced volume and an elongated, thinner shape [40]. These structural changes reflect imbalances in cytoskeletal tension and a diminished capacity to reorganize stress fibres, ultimately disrupting the alignment and coordinated function of muscle fibres [41]. As muscle atrophy progresses, cell size and cross-sectional area are significantly reduced, primarily due to decreased synthesis of structural proteins such as myosin and actin. These changes are not merely passive indicators of atrophy but also active consequences of excessive activation of proteolytic systems, including the ubiquitin–proteasome system (UPS) and the autophagy–lysosome pathway [42]. Concomitantly, the mechanical performance of muscle cells deteriorates. Declines in elasticity and stiffness, often quantified by a reduction in Young’ s modulus, suggest a reduced ability to resist deformation and are typically associated with cytoskeletal disassembly or microtubule network remodelling [43]. An increase in cellular compliance—reflecting the ease with which a cell deforms under external force—further indicates weakening of structural integrity and may compromise the efficiency of mechanotransduction.

In parallel, alterations in cell–matrix interactions further contribute to biomechanical dysfunction. A reduction in adhesion force, driven by impaired integrin signalling and instability of the focal adhesion complex, weakens the physical linkage between muscle cells and the extracellular matrix (ECM). This disruption limits the transmission of mechanical signals to intracellular pathways, such as focal adhesion kinase (FAK) and YAP/TAZ, thereby impairing downstream responses critical for muscle maintenance [44]. Moreover, changes in stress relaxation time and creep rate indicate that atrophic muscle cells exhibit abnormal time-dependent responses to sustained mechanical loading [45,46]. These changes are likely associated with the reorganization of viscoelastic components within the sarcoplasm, including intermediate filaments and myosin clusters, ultimately reducing the cell’s capacity to adapt to dynamic mechanical environments. Additionally, ECM remodelling exerts reciprocal effects on cellular mechanical properties. In the atrophic state, collagen and elastin content is reduced and disorganized, leading to a softer and less structured extracellular matrix. This altered microenvironment affects the mechanical inputs perceived by muscle cells, thereby disrupting the continuum of force transmission from the extracellular space to the cytoskeleton. Together, these structural and mechanical alterations create a self-reinforcing cycle that accelerates muscle degeneration and exacerbates functional loss [47,48].

sec.title: Cytoskeletal remodelling and mechanical signal transduction
sec.p: Muscle atrophy is caused by changes in mechanical signals as well as extensive, linked cytoskeletal rearrangement. Reduced muscular activity or damage to muscle and nerve tissue directly causes these changes, which involve interactions between the cell and its external environment that go beyond internal structures and signalling pathways. This complicated process is regulated by biochemical and biophysical variables, which have an influence on the structure and function of muscle cells [49].

The cytoskeleton, which is mostly composed of actin filaments and microtubules, is responsible for maintaining cellular structure and protecting cells from outside stimuli [50]. Muscle atrophy causes these protein networks to depolymerize and repolymerize, altering their distribution and structure inside the cell. The mechanical stability and contractile capacity of the cell may be compromised, for example, when actin filaments transition from a well-organized to a more disordered state.It can also affect the location of connections in the cell membrane and cytoskeleton. These connections are critical for connecting the cell to the external matrix and receiving external pressure. Integrins are among the molecules involved in these interactions [51,52]. In the case of muscle atrophy, these connections may become weaker or less functional, impairing the transmission of mechanical signals.

To transmit external mechanical stimuli, integrins—transmembrane proteins that connect the cytoskeleton and extracellular matrix—cluster at focal adhesions. Muscle atrophy may result in decreased integrin expression and activity, which impairs focal adhesion function and, in turn, impacts the cell’s capacity to perceive and react to mechanical situations [53]. Focal adhesions transform mechanical inputs into chemical signals, triggering signalling pathways such YAP/TAZ, RhoA, and MAPK [54]. Usually, these routes control the survival, differentiation, and proliferation of cells. These pathways’ activity, however, may be inhibited or aberrantly triggered during muscle atrophy, which might result in further changes to metabolic and cellular processes. Furthermore, within the cell nucleus, changes in mechanical signals might affect gene expression. Genes linked to muscle development and repair may be downregulated and genes that encourage atrophic states may be activated when cytoskeletal reorganization and mechanical signal transmission are interfered with [55,56].

sec.title: Changes in composition and structure of the extracellular matrix
sec.p: The extracellular matrix (ECM) is a complex network of hyaluronic acid, proteoglycans, sticky proteins like fibronectin and laminin, and structural proteins like collagen and elastin.The extracellular matrix (ECM) provides structural support to cells and tissues while also influencing signaling, tissue repair, and cellular activity [57]. In muscle atrophy, the type and quantity of collagen and elastin can alter the protein composition of the extracellular matrix (ECM) [58]. For example, increased collagen content may stiffen the extracellular matrix (ECM), influencing intracellular mechanosignaling via integrin-mediated pathways.

Muscle atrophy may enhance the creation and activity of matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs), enzymes that degrade extracellular matrix components. MMP overactivation can damage the extracellular matrix, altering the physical environment around the cells and influencing their migration, differentiation, and survival [59] (Figure 2).

caption.p: Representation of extracellular matrix (ECM) dynamics in healthy and atrophic muscle conditions. In healthy ECM, collagen fibres, elastic fibres, hyaluronic acid, glycosaminoglycans, and adhesive proteins interact to maintain moderate elasticity, adequate lubrication, and integrin activation, supporting normal muscle function. Conversely, in the atrophic state, elevated matrix metalloproteinase (MMP) activity results in collagen degradation, ECM stiffening, impaired lubrication, and reduced integrin activation, ultimately contributing to muscle fibre atrophy.

sec.title: Influence of the extracellular matrix on the mechanical environment of muscle cells
sec.p: Collagen fibres, the major structural protein of the extracellular matrix (ECM), provide mechanical support to muscle cells during contraction and relaxation. These fibres are critical for conveying muscular forces and are resistant to tensile stress [60].

Muscle cells attach to the extracellular matrix (ECM) and convey mechanical impulses to the outside world through the usage of adhesion proteins such as fibronectin and laminin, which interact with cell surface receptors such as integrins [61]. Muscle cells may adapt to changing mechanical environments by adjusting their internal structure and function in response to mechanical signals. Furthermore, hyaluronic acid and proteoglycans provide a lubricating environment with high humidity, which promotes cell migration and muscle tissue regeneration [62]. The ability of these substances to retain moisture is critical for the overall health of muscle tissue and the promotion of functional recovery.

sec.title: Different types of mechanical stimulation and their regulation of muscle atrophy
sec.p: Mechanosensitivity permits muscle cells to perceive external stressors such as stretching, compressive, and shear stresses via cell surface receptors such as integrins. Cell growth, differentiation, survival, and metabolism are all dependent on the activation of intracellular signaling networks such as the PI3K/Akt signalling pathway [63], the calcium signalling pathway [64], and the MAPK signalling system [65]. Mechanical stimuli, such as compressive, shear, and stretching stresses, affect the expression of a variety of proteins, including cellular response proteins, extracellular matrix proteins, and cytoskeletal proteins. Stretching frequently involves the elongation of cells or tissues, with the application direction parallel to the line of force [66]. On the other hand, compressive stresses act in the opposite direction to stretching stresses and it compresses or shortens cells or tissues [67]. Shear stress, on the other hand, mimics the effects of blood flow on endothelial cells by functioning parallel to the cell surface [68].

Regarding the specificity of biological effects, stretching stress primarily stimulates signaling pathways related to cell extension and growth, whereas compressive stress predominantly influences cellular metabolism and survival pathways, particularly in weight-bearing skeletal muscles. In contrast, shear stress is more prevalent in the vascular system, where its effects are primarily concerned with regulating angiogenesis and endothelial cell function [69]. Table 1 shows the comparison of different mechanical stimuli.

caption.p: Comparison of various mechanical stimuli effect on muscle atrophy.

sec.title: Mechanotransduction pathways regulating muscle atrophy
sec.p: Muscle tissues undergo a sequence of biomechanical and physiological reactions when mechanical stimuli, such stretching, are applied. These external mechanical changes are first detected by mechanoreceptors on the cell surface, such as integrins and other mechanosensors. These receptors initiate certain signalling pathways by activating downstream signalling molecules such Src family kinases and focal adhesion kinase (FAK) [83]. This review focuses on three mechanotransduction pathways that are particularly associated with muscle atrophy (Figure 3).

caption.p: Three mechanical signalling pathways in muscle atrophy.

sec.title: PI3K/akt/mTOR pathway
sec.p: Stretching stimuli can activate the PI3K/Akt signaling pathway, promoting muscle growth by enhancing protein synthesis and inhibiting protein degradation. The activation of Akt, either directly or indirectly, leads to the activation of mTOR (mechanistic target of rapamycin), which further stimulates protein synthesis [84]. Concurrently, Akt inhibits the activity of FoxO transcription factors, thereby preventing the expression of atrophy-related genes such as MuRF1 and atrogin-1. These two proteins function as muscle-specific ubiquitin ligases that are involved in protein degradation [85].

sec.title: MAPK/ERK pathway
sec.p: Stretching and shear forces can activate the MAPK/ERK pathway through the release of fibroblast growth factor (FGF) and other growth factors. The activation of ERK promotes the expression and activation of cyclins, thereby enhancing cell proliferation and growth, which contributes to counteracting muscle atrophy [86].

sec.title: Calcium-dependent signalling pathway
sec.p: Mechanical stimuli induce changes in intracellular calcium levels through mechanosensitive ion channels, such as TRPC channels. This alteration is a crucial event for the activation of various signalling pathways. Calcium-activated signals, such as calcium/calmodulin-dependent kinase, control intracellular signalling and muscle fibre contraction, which affects the ratio of protein synthesis to breakdown [87].

These pathways promote survival, proliferation, and anabolic activities while suppressing the expression of important genes linked to atrophy, so counteracting muscle atrophy brought on by inactivity or illness. Furthermore, extended stretching and frequent shear force stimulation enhance local blood flow and nutrition supply, which supports muscular tissue health and prevents atrophy [88].

sec.title: The application of cellular biomechanics in the treatment of muscle atrophy
sec.p: Cell biomechanics plays a significant role in the development and progression of muscle atrophy. In the treatment of muscle atrophy, non-pharmacological therapies and pharmacotherapy are the main methods currently used to intervene in the process of muscle atrophy. Combined with the principles of cell biomechanics, the aim is to intervene in the process of muscle atrophy through different mechanisms.

Non-pharmacological therapies mainly involve applying mechanical stimulation or other physical factors to directly or indirectly affect the biomechanical environment of muscle cells, thereby achieving therapeutic goals. These therapies primarily include exercise therapy, stem cell therapy, and treatments using new biomaterials and tissue engineering methods (Figure 4).

caption.p: Treatment of muscle atrophy by the use of cellular biomechanics.

sec.p: Pharmacotherapy mainly involves the pharmacological effects of drugs to intervene in the pathological process of muscle atrophy at the molecular level. These methods are highly targeted and act quickly, mainly including calcium channel blockers, β-2 adrenergic agonists, vasodilators, α-adrenergic receptor antagonists, ACE inhibitors, angiotensin II receptor blockers, non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs - NSAIDs, and growth factors and peptide drugs (Table 2).

caption.p: Pharmacological treatment. The names, kinds, methods, and therapeutic uses of medications that alter the biomechanical characteristics of muscle cells are included in this table.

Directly or indirectly promotes muscle growth and inhibits its degradation by activating or enhancing the activity of the mTOR pathway, thereby promoting its repair and regeneration in states of muscle damage or disease.

sec.title: Clinical applications of mechanical interventions
sec.p: For patients experiencing disuse-induced atrophy due to prolonged lack of mechanical load, the most direct intervention is stretching. This involves applying mechanical tension to stiffened myofibers through bed-based traction, passive joint mobilization, or controlled active stretching. This process is analogous to plucking a slack string; it reactivates integrin–FAK/Akt/mTOR signalling, suppresses FoxO-mediated proteolysis, aids in the restoration of fibre cross-sectional area, and recruits satellite cells to facilitate regeneration [98].

In the context of neurogenic atrophy, where muscle fibres lose their connection due to severed neural input, shear-force therapy can be effectively employed. By utilizing c or vibration platforms to apply periodic tangential stress to the muscle, local VEGF expression and Nrf2-driven antioxidant pathways are stimulated, leading to improved microcirculation [99,100]. This subtle lateral friction not only mitigates ROS accumulation following denervation but also enhances mechanotransduction between residual nerves and muscle fibres, thereby slowing the atrophy process despite limited neural support [101].

For myogenic atrophy, which typically arises from intrinsic muscle-cell pathologies such as inflammatory myopathies or age-related sarcopenia, high-intensity mechanical loading may be counterproductive [102]. Instead, low-intensity compression serves as a gentle rain: intermittent pneumatic compression or light-resistance exercise softly activates the AMPK pathway, triggers moderate autophagy to clear damaged organelles, relieves mitochondrial stress, and restores energy homeostasis [103]. This gentle compression sustains myofiber viability and contractile function without inducing excessive proteolysis.

By adhering to this unified logic—matching stretching to disuse, shear forces to neurogenic atrophy, and low-intensity compression to myogenic atrophy—we can customize mechanical interventions to address each aetiology effectively. Selecting the appropriate stimulus modality and intensity allows muscle fibres, each affected by different pathological conditions, to regain strength.

sec.title: Biomechanical mechanisms of exercise in promoting muscle regeneration and repair
sec.p: The growth and repair of muscles depend on satellite cells, which are found on the surface of muscle fibres. Exercise encourages the growth and differentiation of these cells into myofibroblasts, which assists in the healing of damaged muscle tissue. According to Inseon Kim et al. [104], the activation of satellite cells during exercise promotes muscle regeneration and repair by causing microdamage to muscle fibres from muscle contraction, stretching, and resistance training, as well as the accumulation of immune cells and cytokines, growth factors, and metabolic byproducts.

Growth hormones are required to help muscles recover and adapt after exercise. Insulin-like growth factor-1 (IGF-1) is a key growth hormone that influences muscle development and repair. Muscle IGF-1 expression increases after exercise, which initiates the PI3K/Akt signalling cascade. This mechanism promotes protein synthesis and inhibits proteolysis, allowing muscle cells to divide and expand [105]. Tadashi Yoshida’s study [106] demonstrated that IGF-1 activates satellite cells and promotes their maturation into mature muscle cells.

Anna M. Kolkmann et al. [105,106] discovered that hepatocyte growth factor (HGF) and fibroblast growth factor (FGF) can activate satellite cells, promoting their production in order to enhance and restore muscle structure and function. Sébastien Le Garf’s study [107] demonstrated that exercise may balance muscle regeneration and fibrosis by modulating TGF-β activity. Exercise also increases brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF) and nerve growth factor (NGF) [108], which promotes effective nerve-muscle communication and maintains and improves muscular function, according to Almog Katz et al.

Exercise enhances blood circulation and increases blood flow to muscle tissues, which accelerates the elimination of waste products and inflammatory mediators. It also increases oxygen and nutrition availability. Exercise also promotes mitochondrial biogenesis, which is essential for muscle growth and repair and ultimately helps with recovery, increasing the amount of ATP produced in muscles [109]. Muscle cells’ increased activity of antioxidant enzymes after exercise aids in the fight against free radicals produced during physical activity, lowering oxidative stress and its detrimental effects on muscle cells [110].

sec.title: Aerobic exercise
sec.p: Aerobic exercises, such as walking, jogging, and swimming, primarily enhance muscle function by increasing heart rate and blood flow, which boosts the oxygen supply to muscle tissues and facilitates the clearance of metabolic waste products. Studies indicate that regular aerobic exercise can increase the number of capillaries in muscle tissue, improve blood circulation, and enhance the efficiency of oxygen utilization [111]. These physiological changes contribute to increased muscle endurance, allowing muscles to sustain activity for extended periods without fatigue.

Research conducted by Dan Wang et al. has demonstrated that aerobic exercise can promote the increase and functional improvement of mitochondria within muscle cells, thereby accelerating energy production and supply and increasing the muscle metabolic rate [112]. Muscles that regularly engage in aerobic exercise may experience a shift from type II to type I muscle fibres, along with enhanced blood flow within the muscle. This process aids in the more rapid clearance of fatigue-related byproducts, such as lactate, thus reducing recovery time and improving the capacity for sustained, low-to-moderate intensity activity.

Additionally, aerobic exercise enhances the flexibility of muscles and joints, reducing stiffness and contributing to overall biomechanical performance. This not only improves exercise efficiency but also lowers the risk of injury during physical activity.

sec.title: Resistance exercise
sec.p: Research has demonstrated that resistance exercise, including weightlifting and resistance band training, challenges muscles by applying external loads. This type of training induces micro-damage to muscle fibres, which stimulates the proliferation and repair of muscle cells, ultimately leading to an increase in muscle mass and volume (muscle hypertrophy) and enhancing strength and power output [113]. Additionally, resistance exercise increases muscle cross-sectional area and improves neuromuscular coordination, significantly augmenting an individual’s maximum strength and power output capacity.

In contrast to aerobic exercise, Anouk E. Hiensch et al. found that resistance training tends to enhance the size and functionality of type II muscle fibres (fast-twitch fibres), which are particularly crucial for explosive and high-intensity activities, such as jumping and sprinting [114]. Furthermore, Naoki Ito and colleagues demonstrated that resistance training can activate the Focal Adhesion Kinase (FAK) signalling pathway through mechanosensitive receptors, such as integrins, thereby promoting the reorganization of the muscle cell cytoskeleton and stimulating muscle growth [115].

sec.title: Flexibility training
sec.p: Flexibility training, including practices such as yoga and stretching exercises, primarily enhances the flexibility of muscles and joints while improving their range of motion. According to João Pedro Nunes et al. [116], regular flexibility exercises increase the stretch and suppleness of muscle fibres, thus improving muscle performance and efficiency. This training technique reduces stiffness and tightness during exercise and helps to prevent overuse problems and muscle injuries. In addition, according to Aditi Jadhav, flexibility exercises improve blood circulation, which increases the amount of oxygen and nutrients reaching the muscles and other tissues. This process accelerates the removal of metabolic waste and promotes recovery, thereby enhancing overall physical health [117].

sec.title: High-intensity interval training (HIIT)
sec.p: HIIT significantly increases muscle strength and endurance by alternating short bursts of high-intensity exercise with low-intensity rest periods. According to Jose Daniel Jiménez-García [115,116], this training method increases the maximum force generation capacity of the muscle by thickening muscle fibres, especially fast-twitch type II fibres. Another study claimed that high-intensity interval loading enhances muscle oxidative metabolism and increases the number and efficiency of mitochondria in muscle cells [118]. This enhancement improves endurance by allowing muscles to utilize oxygen more efficiently during prolonged or repetitive exercise. In addition, it has been shown that HIIT activates the mTORC1 signalling pathway, which promotes muscle protein synthesis and reduces protein catabolism, thereby accelerating muscle growth and repair [119].

sec.title: The mechanical properties of stem cells and their role in muscle regeneration
sec.p: Stem cells are self-drenewing, undifferentiated cells that can develop into a variety of cell types and are essential for tissue formation and repair [120]. Many factors influence stem cell growth and fate, such as cell shape, intercellular mechanical interactions, and extracellular matrix stiffness [121]. Stem cell development, survival, and function are significantly impacted by a number of important mechanical characteristics.

Because stem cells are morphologically malleable, they may alter their shape to fit the physical features of their surroundings. According to Suttinee Phuagkhaopong and associates, stem cells may become more disseminated on harder substrates and frequently take on a rounder or smaller shape in softer substrates [122]. This morphological adaptability enables stem cells to undergo a variety of mechanical stresses and differentiate into several cell types [123].

Mechanosensitive stem cells can detect the hardness of the substrate they are connected to and change their shape and activity appropriately. Stem cells on stiff substrates were more likely to differentiate into osteoblasts, whereas those on softer substrates were more likely to develop into adipocytes, according to a research by Christopher M. Madl and associates [124]. Furthermore, through certain signalling pathways, stem cells may transform mechanical inputs into biochemical signals. This includes cell surface integrins, ion channels, and other mechanoreceptors that work with the cytoskeleton to regulate gene expression and cellular activity. These interactions lead to stem cell migration through mechanical interactions with the extracellular environment [125].

As emphasized by Quanwei Li [126], the morphology, motility and proliferation of stem cells are directly affected by changes in mechanical signals. Muscle injury triggers the activation, multiplication and gradual differentiation of adult stem cells, especially satellite cells, into new muscle fibres or injured fibres. To restore function, differentiated muscle cells can either fuse with damaged muscle fibres or generate new muscle fibres. According to Shen Yi [121], mechanical properties of the extracellular matrix, biochemical signals and mechanical signals work together to regulate this process. Ensuring that stem cells respond to injury and perform the necessary repair functions depends on proper signalling.

sec.title: The influence of mechanical environment on stem cell differentiation into muscle cells
sec.p: When given the right circumstances and time, stem cells can develop into muscle cells. According to research, outside cues direct this differentiation process. Extracellular growth hormones, cytokines, and other signalling pathways first provide signals to stem cells, which in turn trigger the production of proteins unique to muscles and promote their differentiation into muscle cells [127]. Stem cells have been shown by Stanton et al. to include mechanoreceptors that can sense and react to mechanical signals, including integrins and other extracellular matrix receptors [128].

Multiple intracellular signalling pathways, including the Rho/Rock and MAPK pathways, are activated when mechanical stimuli such stretching, compression, or fluid shear stress are applied. These signals are then transduced via the cell membrane into the interior of the cell [129]. These pathways’ activation affects protein synthesis and gene expression, which in turn affects how stem cells differentiate into muscle cells.

As was already established, stem cells’ morphological elasticity and mechanosensitivity suggest that their destiny is directly influenced by the mechanical stress they endure and the extracellular matrix’s rigidity. According to studies, stem cells can be encouraged to differentiate into skeletal muscle cells by simulating the stiffness of skeletal muscle tissue, which normally ranges from 11 to 17 kPa. On the other hand, differentiation into various cell types may result from softer or stiffer matrices [130]. Research has also shown that three-dimensional culture methods offer a more natural environment for cell development than conventional two-dimensional systems. During muscle cell proliferation, stem cells display more realistic shape and differentiation behavior in three-dimensional environments [131].

sec.title: Strategies of biomaterials simulating mechanical environment of muscle cells
sec.p: An important field in tissue engineering and regenerative medicine is the investigation of methods for simulating the mechanical environment of muscle cells using biomaterials. Biomaterials must mirror the mechanical and physical characteristics of real muscle tissue in order to successfully encourage the differentiation of stem cells into muscle cells. Substrate stiffness has been shown to have a major impact on stem cell fate [129,130]. While substrates of intermediate stiffness, similar to that of muscle tissue, aid in the differentiation of muscle cells, soft substrates, which resemble the stiffness of brain tissue, tend to promote the differentiation of neural cells.

Additionally, growth factors and other signalling molecules that may be released directionally under mechanical pressure can be included into biomaterials to further guide cellular differentiation. Cell shape and mechanical signals can also be influenced by the number and location of cell adhesion sites on the surface of biomaterials [132]. Surface roughness and micro/nanostructures can have a major effect on cell activity, according to research by Yingying Zhang et al. Certain surface designs may be able to replicate the fibre organization of muscle tissue and encourage directed cell development and differentiation [133,134].

Periodic mechanical loading has been shown to enhance muscle cell maturation and functional expression [135]. Martina Culenova’s work demonstrated the utility of bioreactors in applying periodic stretching stimuli, effectively simulating the natural activity of muscle tissue [136]. Additionally, João Gabriel Batista Lage’s research highlighted that in a fluid dynamic environment, cells experience shear stress. This phenomenon, commonly studied in endothelial cells, can also be leveraged in the culture of muscle cells, particularly cardiac muscle cells [137].

Emine Alarcin et al. [138] found that 3D bioprinting technology could construct more complex muscle tissue models, which contain multiple cell types and intricate microstructures, better simulating the real muscle tissue environment. When designing and utilizing specific scaffold materials and structures, it is essential not only to support cell growth and differentiation but also to replicate the mechanical and biological functions of muscle tissue.

sec.title: Cell biomechanics in engineering functional muscle tissues
sec.p: The functional expression of muscle cells is intricately linked to the physical and mechanical properties of their environment. Consideration of a number of biomechanical aspects is crucial in the development and application of muscle tissue engineering techniques, such as substrate stiffness, three-dimensional structure, tissue architecture, mechanical stimulation, the fusion of mechanical and biochemical signals, and intercellular interactions.

The stiffness of the substrate and cell differentiation are strongly connected. According to Anqi Guo’s study [139], selecting materials with the appropriate stiffness is critical for muscle tissue engineering in order to reproduce the physical environment of muscle tissue in its natural state. To optimize muscle cell development and functioning, the material’s stiffness should be similar to that of the target muscle tissue. Furthermore, advances in scaffold design and 3D printing provide precise control over the material’s microstructure, including porosity, pore size, and connectivity [140]. These structural properties aid in cell invasion, development, and nutrient exchange. Fibrous structures and directional printing techniques may also be utilized to replicate the direction and orientation of muscle fibres, which is necessary for stimulating muscle cell proliferation and alignment in certain directions.

Dynamic mechanical stimuli, including compression and stretch, mimic the mechanical environment encountered by muscle tissue during normal activity. This replication can enhance the physiological activity of cells by making them more sensitive to mechanical stimuli [141]. Regular mechanical stimulation has been shown to enhance the function and morphology of muscle tissue. According to G. Cidonio and colleagues [142], mechanical stimulation in combination with biochemical cues (e.g. growth factor release) can more closely approximate the cellular environment in a normal setting, thereby enhancing cellular endurance and contractile efficacy. Furthermore, the connection between different cell types in muscle tissue is crucial. According to Tina Tylek [143], functional integration and repair of tissues depends on the interaction between muscle cells and fibroblasts. In tissue engineering, it is important to consider ways to optimize these cellular interactions through the use of materials and biosignals.

sec.title: Pharmacological treatment
sec.p: Pharmacotherapy is crucial for tissue healing, shoulder function enhancement, and pain alleviation. Through a variety of modes of action, several medications have been demonstrated in recent years to successfully reduce the discomfort associated with rotator cuff injuries and encourage recovery. Calcium channel blockers, β2-adrenergic agonists, vasodilators, ACE inhibitors, and angiotensin II receptor blockers all enhance local blood flow, lower discomfort, and change inflammatory responses, all of which help to minimize the clinical symptoms of rotator cuff problems.By lowering inflammation and promoting tissue repair, growth factors, peptide drugs, and non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) also actively support treatment. The table below provides a useful clinical therapeutic reference by compiling the mechanisms of action of several drugs used to treat rotator cuff problems.

sec.title: Cell biomechanics-based diagnostic methods for muscle atrophy
sec.p: The concepts of cell biomechanics are examined in relation to muscle atrophy, with particular attention paid to alterations in intracellular structures, metabolic processes, and mechanical signals. Muscle atrophy is frequently associated with a decrease in the internal tension of muscle fibers, which leads to alterations in cellular signalling that affect the equilibrium between protein synthesis and degradation. Non-invasive mechanical detection technologies grounded in the principles of cell biomechanics—such as electromyography (EMG), muscle strength testing, ultrasound elastography, magnetic resonance elastography, along with various cellular and molecular biology techniques—can be utilized to diagnose muscle atrophy. By integrating traditional clinical diagnostic methods with these advanced technologies, it is possible to alleviate patient suffering while enhancing diagnostic accuracy and treatment efficacy.

sec.title: Non-invasive mechanical testing technology
sec.p: In recent years, non-invasive mechanical testing techniques have gradually become an important means of diagnosing muscle atrophy due to their non-invasive, real-time, and efficient characteristics. Ultrasound elastography and magnetic resonance elastography can quantitatively analyze the stiffness of muscle tissue by assessing changes in muscle hardness, thereby reflecting the degree of muscle atrophy. Near-infrared spectroscopy and photoacoustic imaging use different wavelengths of light signals for imaging. By detecting blood flow and oxygenation in muscles, they provide indirect diagnostic information for atrophy. Surface electrical impedance imaging provides electrophysiological features of muscle status by measuring changes in electrical resistance on the surface of the muscle. The following table summarizes the principles, advantages and disadvantages, and application situations of these non-invasive mechanical testing techniques, providing strong technical support for the diagnosis and monitoring of muscle atrophy (Table 3).

caption.p: Non-invasive mechanical testing technology. This table summarizes the concepts and measurement parameters for five non-invasive mechanical testing techniques: ultrasonic elastography, magnetic resonance elastography, near-infrared spectroscopy, photoacoustic imaging, and surface impedance imaging.

tr.td: By applying a slight external force to the surface of the tissue, and then using ultrasound to detect the response of the tissue to these forces, the elastic modulus of the tissue can be calculated.

A non-invasive technique based on magnetic resonance imaging, which generates low-frequency vibrations (usually through an external driver) and uses MRI scans to detect the tissue’s response to these vibrations.

By emitting near-infrared light towards the tissue and measuring the reflection or transmission of the light, the blood flow, oxygen content, and other physiological characteristics of the tissue are assessed.
Combining optical and ultrasonic technologies, the system irradiates tissues with laser pulses and monitors the acoustic signals generated.
Infer the structure and mechanical properties by applying low-frequency currents and measuring the electrical impedance (or conductance) of the tissue.

sec.p: In recent years, there has been a lot of interest in using non-invasive mechanical detection approaches in clinical practice, particularly in the areas of muscle atrophy, tissue pathological changes, and disease monitoring. These approaches allow you to examine the mechanical properties of cells or tissues with little or no material.

sec.title: The potential of mechanical parameters as biomarkers for muscle atrophy
sec.p: Muscle atrophy may be identified with more precision, and tailored treatment plans can be developed utilizing cellular biomechanics. Mechanical parameter changes can serve as biomarkers for muscle atrophy throughout the diagnostic process, providing valuable information on pathological abnormalities, muscle mass, and function. Muscle thickness and cross-sectional area, which indicate muscle volume and strength, are commonly measured using ultrasonography or magnetic resonance imaging methods to assess muscle atrophy [152]. Regular monitoring of these parameters can help follow the progression of the disease and assess the efficacy of treatment, as muscular atrophy is directly related to a decrease in muscle volume.

Jae-Young Lim et al. [153] found that muscular stiffness is an important early indicator for tracking or anticipating muscle loss. Tissue hardening is commonly associated with muscular atrophy, and inflexible muscles exhibit greater fibrosis or degenerative changes. Muscular stiffness refers to muscles’ resistance to deformation under external pressure. Magnetic resonance elastography and ultrasonic elastography are two non-invasive procedures for assessing muscle stiffness changes. Yang Sun et al. [154] stress the need of monitoring changes in muscle tissue’s elasticity modulus in order to evaluate muscular atrophy and recovery. The elasticity modulus of a material is used to determine its elastic properties, since it depicts the relationship between stress and strain within its elastic range [155].

When examining muscular atrophy, the elasticity modulus provides a quantitative way to assess muscle condition. Shear wave velocity, or the rate at which shear waves flow through muscle tissue, is a measure of muscle stiffness and elasticity that is closely connected to the tissue’s mechanical properties [156]. Muscle atrophy can be detected by recording changes in mechanical properties caused by fibrosis or atrophy. Slavcho Ivanoski et al.’s study [157] also examines tissue sound velocity. Differences in sound velocity between healthy and sick muscles can be used to infer changes in muscle tissue composition, such as fat infiltration or fibrosis. Muscle mass is one of the several ways that bioelectrical impedance technology is used in body composition studies [158]. Because this approach uses microcurrents to assess the conductivity and capacitance properties of muscle tissue, it is especially well-suited for identifying changes brought on by atrophy, such as the replacement of muscle with fat and connective tissue. Muscle composition and tissue deterioration are intimately associated with these electrical characteristics. In order to forecast when muscle atrophy will start and to create individualized treatment plans, these mechanical features can swiftly and non-invasively provide details on the composition and functionality of muscles. Measurement circumstances, operator expertise, and equipment precision all affect how accurate and useful these measures are in practical applications. Standardized operating methods and further clinical testing are therefore crucial.

sec.title: Cell biomechanics treatment for muscle atrophy
Exercise therapy
sec.p: As previously discussed, exercise therapy can mitigate muscle atrophy through various mechanisms, including the activation of satellite cells, the promotion of growth factor secretion, the enhancement of blood circulation and nutrient supply, the improvement of neuromuscular connections, and the reduction of oxidative stress. Currently, clinically recommended exercise therapies encompass resistance training to enhance strength, which directly stimulates muscle fibers; aerobic training to improve overall metabolism and blood circulation; and functional training—such as walking and stretching—to restore muscle coordination. Collectively, these approaches can help alleviate muscle atrophy to varying degrees. Linda Heskamp et al. conducted a prospective experiment [159] to investigate whether muscle atrophy or fat infiltration improved in 14 patients with type 1 myotonic dystrophy (DM1) following behavioural interventions, utilizing longitudinal quantitative muscle magnetic resonance imaging to evaluate muscle treatment. The results indicated that after increasing physical activity, both the lower limb muscle cross-sectional area and muscle strength of the patients improved. Similarly, Laura Girard-Côté studied 12 women with DM1 [160] and, after 12 weeks of resistance training, performed muscle strength assessments, functional tests, and muscle biopsies. The findings revealed enhancements in muscle strength, function, and muscle cross-sectional area for all 12 patients.

sec.title: Stem cell therapy
sec.p: Scientists at the Klara Medical Center in Poland have reported promising results in the treatment of muscular dystrophy using umbilical mesenchymal stem cells [161]. Among 22 patients studied, 6 (27.3%) exhibited significant improvements in clinical parameters. Belle Yu-Hsuan Wang demonstrated in animal experiments [162] that adipose-derived mesenchymal stem cells, via the ERK1/2 signalling pathway, increase the number of type I muscle fibres, enhance muscle strength, reduce recovery time, and improve fatigue resistance, effectively reversing muscle atrophy. Additionally, researchers from the iPS Cell Research and Application Center (CiRA) at Kyoto University in Japan, led by Aya Harada, found in animal studies [163] that mesenchymal stem cells derived from induced pluripotent stem cells (iMSCs) can systemically restore collagen VI synthesis and promote muscle regeneration in mice with congenital muscular dystrophy. Muscle atrophy is a progressive condition, and exercise therapy has traditionally been employed to treat it, improving symptoms by activating muscle stem cells and regulating the muscle internal environment. In contrast, stem cell therapy offers a novel treatment approach by facilitating muscle regeneration and maturation, replacing damaged nerve cells, and alleviating functional impairments. The integration of these two modalities shows promise for enhancing therapeutic outcomes.

sec.title: Challenges and future directions in diagnosis and treatment
sec.p: In the diagnosis and treatment of muscle atrophy, cellular biomechanics plays an increasingly important role, with core issues focusing on the response mechanisms of cells to mechanical signals, optimization of the mechanical microenvironment, model construction, and clinical translation. Although research has shown [164] that muscle cells and stem cells perceive mechanical signals through integrins, ion channels, and cytoskeletal proteins, the precise regulatory network for these mechanisms has not been fully elucidated. A key scientific question remains how mechanical stimulation affects the differentiation, migration, proliferation, and self-renewal of muscle cells. Furthermore, mechanical stimulation at different time points, frequencies, and directions may trigger different cellular behaviours [165]. Most current studies are limited to static or singular mechanical parameters, and the specific effects of dynamic mechanical stimulation have not been fully revealed.

Pathological tissues in muscle atrophy are often accompanied by fibrosis, inflammation, and oxidative stress, all of which create a complex environment that may impair the cells’ ability to perceive mechanical signals and even induce abnormal responses. Additionally, how mechanical stimuli interact with biochemical signals, such as growth factors and cytokines, to promote muscle regeneration is still unclear. This may involve the interaction of multiple signalling pathways, creating a complex network, but there is a lack of specific experimental data to support this hypothesis.

Most in vitro research uses crude 2D culture methods for experimental models, which fall short in simulating the complex 3D extracellular matrix and its mechanical characteristics present in genuine tissues. Additionally, there are noticeable differences between humans and animal models like mice and rats in terms of metabolic levels, tissue elasticity, and muscle structure [166]. These differences pose critical concerns concerning the practical applicability of cellular biomechanics research. Stretchable platforms and microfluidic devices—instruments used in cellular biomechanics research—continue to face cost and accuracy issues, limiting their use in clinical settings. Furthermore, the long-term therapeutic benefits of mechanical stimulation are debatable, necessitating the employment of alternative treatment techniques such as stem cell therapy or pharmacological treatments.

Future research should concentrate on extensively investigating the mechanisms that govern cellular mechanosensing and response. The molecular mechanisms of ion channels involved in YAP/TAZ signalling, the integrin-focal adhesion pathway, and the perception of mechanical stimuli should be given specific attention. Future study should focus on how these systems work together to regulate muscle regeneration. Furthermore, it is critical to investigate how mechanical signals might increase muscle cell function by controlling dynamic cytoskeleton changes such as actin remodeling and microtubule network modifications. More accurate simulations of muscle cell activity inside 3D mechanical microenvironments may be achieved by combining biomimetic materials and 3D printing technology to build 3D environments that closely match real muscle tissues [167]. To accelerate the practical translation of cellular biomechanics therapies, more collaboration across cell biology, engineering, clinical medicine, and sports science is necessary. Muscle atrophy can be treated more effectively when combined with medicine, stem cell treatments, and other intervention strategies.

sec.title: Conclusion and outlook
sec.p: Because muscle atrophy is a complicated and multidimensional condition, it presents a considerable challenge in the realm of biomedical research. Using cellular biomechanics, this work emphasizes the significance of mechanical signals in muscle atrophy as well as their potential and future aspirations. It has been shown that muscle cells use the cytoskeleton, integrins, and mechanosensitive channels to sense the mechanical environment outside of them. These cells directly affect muscle development and breakdown by activating signaling pathways such as mTOR, PI3K/Akt, and YAP/TAZ.Furthermore, mechanical stimuli impact cellular communication and the tissue microenvironment by modulating the extracellular matrix (ECM) and the release of inflammatory factors, which can either exacerbate or mitigate the atrophic process. At the single-cell level, key biomechanical parameters—such as stiffness, elasticity, and adhesion—are significantly altered during muscle atrophy, indicating a deterioration in structural integrity and mechanosensing capacity. These alterations contribute to disrupted signal transduction pathways and may accelerate the degenerative process. Based on these mechanisms, external mechanical interventions—including low-frequency vibration, mechanical stretching, and biomimetic muscle stimulation devices—are emerging as promising strategies for the treatment of muscle atrophy.

Despite notable advancements in the understanding of cellular biomechanics related to muscle atrophy, several limitations persist. Firstly, the complexity and variability of the mechanical microenvironment have not been fully replicated, and existing experimental models (such as two-dimensional cultures or animal studies) exhibit significant discrepancies from the human body, complicating the accurate prediction of treatment outcomes. Secondly, the molecular mechanisms remain incompletely elucidated, and the specific effects of various mechanical parameters (such as frequency, intensity, and direction) on muscle cell function necessitate further in-depth investigation. Furthermore, the clinical application of mechanical interventions is currently constrained by a lack of systematic large-scale clinical trials and long-term efficacy assessments. Future research should prioritize the following directions (1); conducting an in-depth analysis of the mechanisms that govern the interaction between cellular mechanics and muscle cell differentiation and regeneration, thereby establishing a comprehensive theoretical foundation (2); developing more efficient and precise mechanical stimulation devices, along with biomimetic materials tailored for personalized treatment (3); facilitating the translation of laboratory research findings into clinical applications to enhance treatment efficacy and broaden the patient population.

In conclusion, the application of cellular biomechanics in treating muscle atrophy offers a novel perspective for addressing the limitations of traditional treatments and establishes a critical groundwork for the advancement of regenerative medicine and precision medicine. As research and technological innovation continue to progress, cellular biomechanics is anticipated to play a pivotal role in the treatment of muscle atrophy and other degenerative diseases, thereby expanding the horizons of medical applications.
